project_reference.core_research_question: What are the erasure, depolarizing, and biased-noise thresholds of holographic quantum codes, and which code families approach or exceed known channel benchmarks?
project_reference.current_focus: Bootstrap literature-backed roadmap for threshold studies in HaPPY, heptagon/Steane, and zero-rate holographic codes.

scope.question: How do published erasure, depolarizing, and biased-noise thresholds compare across representative holographic quantum code families, and what unresolved frontier remains after normalizing for decoder and code-rate regime?
in_scope: Assemble a primary-source threshold map for HaPPY-related, heptagon/Steane, SCF, and related holographic stabilizer codes
Normalize each published threshold by decoder type, noise model, and zero-rate versus finite-rate regime
Identify the most defensible open problem for a follow-on threshold study
out_of_scope: Circuit-level fault-tolerant thresholds with noisy syndrome extraction
Implementing a new decoder or reproducing Monte Carlo data in this session
Non-stabilizer or approximate holographic codes beyond cited stabilizer benchmarks
unresolved_questions: Do any finite-rate holographic codes have published biased-noise threshold maps comparable to the new zero-rate results?
Which decoder and noise-model normalizations are strict enough for apples-to-apples comparison across the current literature?

context_gaps: Finite-rate biased-noise threshold evidence remains unclear from the initial literature sweep
crucial_inputs: Threshold values must be tagged by channel, decoder, and zero-rate versus finite-rate regime before comparison

formulations: Tensor-network stabilizer-code comparison across holographic families
allowed_estimator_families: Finite-size threshold crossing arguments as reported by source papers
forbidden_estimator_families: Single fixed-size non-detectable-error proxies treated as thresholds
Cross-paper comparisons that ignore decoder or channel mismatch
allowed_fit_families: Threshold comparisons only when the source paper states the observable and decoder explicitly
stop_and_rethink_conditions: A newer primary source already closes the finite-rate biased-noise gap
Quoted threshold values turn out not to be comparable because decoder or channel definitions differ materially

observables.name: Erasure threshold
observables.definition: Published erasure threshold or lower bound for protected bulk or central logical qubits in a holographic code family

observables.name: Depolarizing threshold
observables.definition: Published code-capacity depolarizing threshold for a representative holographic code family under a named decoder

observables.name: Biased-noise performance class
observables.definition: Whether a holographic code family reaches or exceeds the hashing bound in a stated biased Pauli-noise regime

claims.statement: Produce a primary-source threshold map for representative holographic code families, separating erasure, depolarizing, and biased-noise results by decoder and code-rate regime.

Within the inspected anchor set, isolate the strongest unresolved frontier as the gap between strong zero-rate biased-noise results and the still-missing finite-rate biased-noise threshold map under matched decoder and observable conventions.

deliverables.description: Final research note summarizing threshold benchmarks, cross-paper comparison discipline, and the open frontier.
must_contain: channel and decoder normalized threshold table

deliverables.description: Phase roadmap for a follow-on threshold investigation.

acceptance_tests.subject: claim-threshold-map
acceptance_tests.procedure: Check every threshold statement in the report against one of the must-read primary references and label the channel, decoder, and rate regime.
acceptance_tests.pass_condition: No threshold statement lacks a primary reference or omits decoder/channel context.

acceptance_tests.subject: claim-threshold-map
acceptance_tests.procedure: Verify that any cross-paper comparison is made only after the report names the decoder and noise-model context.
acceptance_tests.pass_condition: Comparisons only appear with explicit decoder and channel qualifiers; unresolved mismatches remain flagged.

acceptance_tests.subject: claim-frontier-gap
acceptance_tests.procedure: Review the final report to confirm that the unresolved frontier is stated together with the weakest anchor and the main competing explanation.
acceptance_tests.pass_condition: The report names a concrete frontier gap, the weakest anchor supporting it, and what result would overturn it.

references.why_it_matters: Introduces holographic QECCs and establishes the erasure-threshold baseline for HaPPY-like constructions.

references.locator: Harris et al., Phys. Rev. A 98, 052301 (2018), arXiv:1806.06472
references.why_it_matters: Introduces the CSS heptagon holographic code and its erasure-decoder benchmark.

references.locator: Harris et al., Phys. Rev. A 102, 062417 (2020), arXiv:2008.10206
references.why_it_matters: Provides phenomenological Pauli-error thresholds across holographic code families and makes code-rate dependence explicit.

references.locator: Farrelly et al., Phys. Rev. A 105, 052446 (2022), arXiv:2012.07317
references.why_it_matters: Sharpens depolarizing and dephasing threshold benchmarks using tensor-network decoding, including zero-rate performance.
Provides the strongest inspected zero-rate benchmark for holographic codes under biased Pauli noise and hashing-bound comparisons.

forbidden_proxies.subject: claim-threshold-map
forbidden_proxies.proxy: Treating fixed-size non-detectable-error probability as a true threshold without finite-size threshold crossings.
forbidden_proxies.reason: Some holographic-code studies report proxies or bulk-specific thresholds that are not interchangeable with asymptotic threshold statements.

forbidden_proxies.subject: claim-threshold-map
forbidden_proxies.proxy: Comparing thresholds from different decoders or different noise channels as if they measured the same benchmark.
forbidden_proxies.reason: Decoder and channel mismatch can dominate the apparent performance ordering.

weakest_anchors: Whether any finite-rate holographic code has a published biased-noise threshold map comparable to the zero-rate results
unvalidated_assumptions: Threshold values reported across different papers remain meaningfully comparable after decoder normalization
competing_explanations: Apparent superiority of zero-rate biased-noise results may partly reflect decoder choice and channel specialization rather than intrinsic holographic geometry
disconfirming_observations: A published finite-rate biased-noise study matching or surpassing the current zero-rate hashing-bound results would weaken the claimed frontier gap

intermediate_results.description: Core threshold anchor set fixed to Pastawski 2015, Harris 2018, Harris 2020, Farrelly 2022, and Fan arXiv:2408.06232v3 for this project.
Pastawski et al. (2015) established the canonical HaPPY-style erasure-threshold benchmark and the threshold picture that later holographic-code papers compare against.
intermediate_results.validity: erasure channel; HaPPY-style tensor-network holographic code

intermediate_results.description: Harris et al. (2018) introduced the CSS heptagon holographic code and reported a stronger erasure-channel benchmark than the earlier mixed pentagon/hexagon family under an optimal erasure decoder.
intermediate_results.validity: erasure channel; central logical qubit recovery with optimal erasure decoder

intermediate_results.description: Harris et al. (2020) found phenomenological Pauli-error thresholds ranging from 7% to 16%, with the range depending on code rate.
intermediate_results.validity: Pauli-error phenomenology with integer-optimization decoding for bulk qubits

intermediate_results.description: Farrelly et al. (2022) computed a 9.4% bulk depolarizing threshold for the max-rate holographic Steane code using a parallel tensor-network decoder.
intermediate_results.validity: depolarizing noise; bulk logical qubits; max-rate holographic Steane code

intermediate_results.description: Fan et al., arXiv:2408.06232v3 (submitted 2024; revised 2025), found that all tested zero-rate holographic codes reach the hashing bound in some bias regime, with several constructions surpassing prior 2-Pauli-noise benchmarks.
intermediate_results.validity: biased Pauli noise; zero-rate holographic code families under tensor-network decoding

intermediate_results.description: Within the inspected anchor set, the next decisive missing comparison is a finite-rate biased-noise threshold map under decoder-controlled comparisons.

open_questions: Do any finite-rate holographic codes have published biased-noise threshold maps comparable to the new zero-rate results?
Which decoder and noise-model normalizations are strict enough for apples-to-apples comparison across the current literature?
How should central-logical, bulk-logical, and full-code thresholds be normalized across holographic-code papers before direct comparison?
Are the strongest biased-noise gains generic to holographic geometry, or are they currently specific to zero-rate constructions and tailored seed tensors?

decisions.summary: Treat decoder, channel, and logical-qubit location as hard comparison boundaries for threshold claims.
decisions.rationale: The cited literature mixes erasure, depolarizing, and biased-noise channels plus different decoders and observables; collapsing them would overstate the evidence.

approximations.name: Cross-paper threshold comparability
approximations.validity_range: Valid only when noise channel, decoder, and logical-qubit observable are aligned

approximations.name: Bulk-threshold proxy
approximations.validity_range: Useful only when the source paper explicitly studies logical qubits at fixed bulk depth

convention_lock.metric_signature: mostly plus (-,+,+,+) when relativistic notation appears; otherwise code-level Pauli/stabilizer notation
convention_lock.natural_units: Thresholds reported as dimensionless physical error probabilities; use hbar=c=1 only when translating holographic/AdS notation from source papers
convention_lock.coordinate_system: Hyperbolic-disk / Schlaefli-tiling geometry; layers indexed by graph distance from the central logical qubit
